text_blocks.text: Wilhelm Erb's (1874)
"intensive" theory, that a pain signal can be
generated by intense enough stimulation of
any sensory receptor, has been soundly
disproved. Some sensory fibers do not
differentiate between noxious and non-noxious
stimuli, while others, nociceptors, respond
only to noxious, high intensity stimuli. At
the peripheral end of the nociceptor, noxious
stimuli generate currents that, above a
given threshold, begin to send signals along
the nerve fiber to the spinal cord. The
"specificity" (whether it responds to thermal,
chemical or mechanical features of its
environment) of a nociceptor is determined
by which ion channels it expresses at its
peripheral end. Dozens of different types of
nociceptor ion channels have so far been
identified, and their exact functions are
still being determined.
